oai_dc:dc.dc:title: Dealing with Mycoplasma bovis-associated disease in dairy calves
oai_dc:dc.dc:creator: Maunsell, Fiona
oai_dc:dc.dc:subject: Mycoplasma bovis
respiratory disease
dairy
oai_dc:dc.dc:description: Mycoplasma bovis is an important contributor to morbidity in dairy calves, where it is associated with respiratory disease as well as other clinical presentations such as otitis media and arthritis. Subclinical, intermittent shedding is common and is likely responsible for maintenance of M. bovis in dairy popula­tions. Transmission occurs mainly through contact with re­spiratory secretions or milk from infected cattle, and feeding unpasteurized waste milk can cause outbreaks of M. bovis-as­sociated disease. Control of clinical disease in calf populations requires a broad approach, focusing on both general risk fac­tors for respiratory disease as well as M. bovis-specific factors, and prompt identification and treatment of clinical cases with appropriate antimicrobials. Independent data is lacking on new vaccines, but these vaccines may prove helpful in M. bovis con­trol in some dairy calf populations.
oai_dc:dc.dc:source: American Association of Bovine Practitioners Conference Proceedings; Vol. 58 No. 2 (2025): 2025 AABP Annual Conference Proceedings; 87-91
oai_dc:dc.dc:rights: Copyright (c) 2026 American Association of Bovine Practitioners
